data.title: Do Brown-Forman's Q1 Earnings Justify Re-Entering The Stock?
data.description:
Introduction

Shares of Brown-Forman Corporation (NYSE:BF.A, NYSE:BF.B), the company behind Jack Daniel's whiskey, have had a wild ride so far in 2025 and, more generally, have been shunned by the market for several years now.

I have reported on Brown-Forman shares several times here on Seeking Alpha and have always found them too expensive, especially given the company's weak growth. However, in my last article, published in February after BF.A/BF.B shares declined to the low $30 range, I explained why I ultimately thought it made sense to assume a cautious bullish stance. Given the continuing high level of uncertainty, however, I advocated an options-based strategy. I successfully traded BF.A put options over the last six months and was not assigned any shares (although I would not have minded given the strike prices of as little as $25). I bought back my last contracts during the rally ahead of the release of fiscal 2026 first-quarter results, which were published yesterday.

In contrast to the results for the fourth quarter and full year 2025, it appears that the market did not like Brown-Forman's start to the new fiscal year. Shares fell by around 5%, dropping below $30 again, which raises the question of whether I should re-enter the stock and, if so, directly via share ownership or instead once again via put options.

In this article, I will, therefore, provide an overview of Brown-Forman's latest earnings report, but given that I last covered the company in February, I will also give my assessment of the Jack Daniel's manufacturer's fiscal 2025, which ended in July.

Brown-Forman Q1 FY2026 Earnings Review And My Longer-Term View

In the first quarter of fiscal 2026, Brown-Forman generated net sales of $924 million, which was slightly better than expected but still represents a 3% decline compared to the previous year (Figure 1). However, organic net sales rose 1% year-over-year, although this should not be interpreted as a sign of recovery, as the company expects organic sales to decline in the low single digits for the full year.

Operating earnings declined 7% and diluted earnings per share declined 13% year-over-year, slightly below consensus. However, it is important to note that the result was not influenced by one-off effects and Brown-Forman refrained from reporting adjusted earnings.

The ongoing weakness in earnings (the reasons for which I explained in detail in my previous coverage) is naturally disappointing, but I was nonetheless pleased to see that gross margin was maintained at almost 60% and even recovered by 33 basis points compared to the previous year (Figure 2). Given the easy comparison and the seasonality of the business, the sequential gross margin expansion of 236 basis points should not, of course, be overrated.

Brown-Forman is clearly maintaining good cost control despite the current difficult environment. In addition to the resilient gross margin, it is worth noting that selling, general, and administrative expenses for the quarter were $177 million, which is in line with the five-year average but represents a 6% decrease year-over-year. Advertising expenses declined 5% year-over-year -which isn't necessarily positive - but as can be seen in Figure 3, they remain in line with historical trends, even relatively speaking as a percentage of net sales. Given the current unfavorable demand trend and product innovation, I would like to see an increase in advertising spending, even if it comes at additional expense of profits.

Taken together, the (modest) gross margin expansion and the decline in selling, general, and administrative expenses and advertising spending should have led to an increase in operating margin, but instead there was a decline of 1.4 percentage points. The main reasons for this were restructuring costs (which were not excluded from reported income) and lower other income (partly due to currency derivatives).

Finally, the reason for the double-digit decline in earnings per share (compared to a 7% decline in operating income) was a postretirement expense of $19 million, which was slightly offset by lower interest expenses and lower income taxes. The number of shares used to calculate earnings per share remained virtually unchanged from the previous year, so dilution from stock options did not play a role.

Given the difficult demand environment and, most recently, also the tariffs situation, it is clear that investing in Brown-Forman shares requires patience. This is also why I continue to approach the investment via put options. I think it says a lot that current EPS expectations for fiscal 2026 are almost identical to Brown-Forman's earnings for fiscal 2018 (Figure 4), and it doesn't take much imagination to see that the stock is likely to remain what is often referred to as "dead money" for the foreseeable future.

For those who follow Brown-Forman and its peers, it likely comes as no surprise that inventories remain extremely high, both in absolute terms and as a percentage of trailing twelve months net sales (Figure 5). However, given the specifics of Brown-Forman's business, I doubt that the company will have to take significant inventory-related impairment charges going forward.

That said, finished goods inventory has grown at an above-average rate over the last two quarters, up 8% from the fourth quarter and 20% from the third quarter of fiscal 2025, which is obviously putting pressure on profits and cash flows. However, management is committed to reduce the surplus in due course. At the same time, while this increase is certainly noteworthy, the general trend of an increase in finished goods from the third quarter onwards is normal. After all, the third quarter includes the holiday season.

Given the ongoing operational challenges and high inventory levels (which impact cash flow through tied-up working capital), it is entirely justified to take a fresh look at Brown-Forman's debt and free cash flow. This also applies with regard to the dividend (current starting yield 3.1%), which is likely to be the only significant return factor over the near term.

From a cash flow perspective, fiscal 2026 started off rather well for the company, with operating cash flow of $160 million, driven by a decline in accounts receivable and lower inventory additions than in the same period last year. Similarly, payables and accruals increased less than over the first quarter of fiscal 2025. That said, in light of the dynamics throughout the year and Brown-Forman's seasonality, I wouldn't overemphasize the good cash inflow from operations in Q1.

Hence, and while these observations leave a positive impression, it is worth taking a look at Brown-Forman's performance in fiscal 2025. Remember that after the post-pandemic recovery, free cash flow declined from the typical $600 million to $800 million per year to only around $400 million in fiscal 2023. I was glad to see that free cash flow for fiscal 2025 came in at $403 million, after adjusting for stock-based compensation (Figure 6). Note that I have refrained from smoothing out the impact of working capital, as it is currently uncertain how long it will take Brown-Forman to bring its inventories back to normal levels.

Brown-Forman currently pays out its entire free cash flow in dividends, and even its average payout ratio over the last four years is 100% in terms of free cash flow. Although the company generally pays out most or all of its free cash flow - as demonstrated for example by the special dividend in fiscal 2022 - I would prefer to see a slightly more conservative approach in light of the current environment. At the same time, I appreciate management's commitment to maintaining (and even increasing) the dividend despite the current headwinds.

From a leverage perspective, I would argue that this behavior is not fully justified. Net debt remains comparatively high at $2.3 billion (Figure 7), representing almost six times current free cash flow. This leaves little or no room for acquisitions, while at the same time the company is currently unable to repay any debt due to its 100% payout ratio. However, given that the conventionally calculated leverage ratio is only about 2x net debt to EBITDA for fiscal 2025, I would not overemphasize the issue from a creditor's perspective. Also, Brown-Forman still has an A1 credit rating (Moody's), but the rating agency has – quite understandably – changed the outlook to negative back in late 2024 due to the elevated leverage.

Personally, I consider the dividend reasonably safe for now, but long-term investors need to factor in the risk of a rebasement if the demand environment does not improve significantly in the coming years and interest rates do not decline as expected. Of course, management could buy more time by monetizing assets (as for example in fiscal 2025, $350 million), but that is clearly not something I want to see on a regular basis, especially in a difficult market environment. I would much rather prefer to see such transactions during boom phases.

Conclusion: Why I Maintain My Options-Based Strategy With Brown-Forman Stock

Brown-Forman Corporation continues to operate in a challenging demand environment, but I would argue that the results for the first quarter of fiscal 2026 are as good as they could be given the circumstances. I was particularly pleased to see gross margin remain stable, which is a key pillar of my investment thesis for Brown-Forman. As shown on slide 7 of the earnings presentation, gross margin remained stable despite a slight currency headwind, mainly thanks to portfolio optimizations. It is important to note that pricing and mix did not contribute to gross margin expansion in the first quarter, underscoring the strength of the portfolio in light of organic top-line performance.

Also noteworthy is the continued good cost control in sales, general, and administrative expenses and advertising expenditures, although I would like to see a temporary focus on above-average advertising specifically targeting ready-to-drink products and newly introduced flavored high-alcohol products (e.g., Jack Daniel's Blackberry and other flavors).

While sales in the domestic market and in developed international markets were disappointing as expected (down 2% and 9% year-over-year organically, respectively), the trend in emerging markets was promising (up 25% year-over-year organically). And although this segment still only makes a small contribution to Brown-Forman's top-line, I am nevertheless very pleased that this traditionally U.S.-focused company is increasingly turning its attention to emerging markets, which I believe (alongside product innovations) are the most important growth driver going forward.

Given the difficult demand environment and, most recently, the tariffs situation, it is rather obvious that investing in Brown-Forman shares requires patience. Free cash flow is likely to remain subdued also in fiscal 2026, and it will most certainly take years for the company to return to sustainable free cash flow of well over $600 million annually. This implies a forward free cash flow yield of 4.4%, which I consider reasonable for rating BF.A/BF.B shares a cautious buy. The forward P/E ratio of slightly below 18 points in the same direction, although Brown-Forman stock is certainly not trading at a significant discount to fair value (see my previous article) and its longer-term earnings performance is definitely disappointing.

For these reasons, I believe Brown-Forman stock could remain "dead money" for the foreseeable future (aside from the 3% dividend yield). Therefore, I will continue to approach this investment through put options. I plan to sell put options expiring on October 17 or December 19 with strike prices of $25 and $27.50, as I am not overly optimistic about Brown-Forman's share price in the short term. If I am assigned the shares, I would not mind at the implied valuation, but at the same time, I can see myself continuing to trade options and earning the reasonable returns associated with them. For example, the put expiring on December 19 with a strike of $27.50 was trading at $1.60 shortly before yesterday's close, which corresponds to an implied annualized return of 20%.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: CLM: Unique High-Yield Income Opportunity, But Only For Active Investors
data.description:
Overview

I'd argue that Cornerstone Strategic Investment Fund (NYSE:CLM) is one of the most unique income funds available to investors today. While the high yield is what may attract most people initially, there is another benefit to this fund that can compound your wealth and total returns rapidly over time. When I previously covered this fund, I issued a buy rating and I briefly talked about this benefit. However, I've seen lots of confusion and misunderstanding about how CLM operates so I wanted to revisit this fund to lay out the exact blueprint on how to properly utilize it.

While the long term performance chart of CLM may look scary, there's a perfectly logical reason why CLM's share price has a long history of declines. As we can see below, CLM's share price has declined by 52.3% over the last ten years. However, the total return jumps up to 232.5% when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders. At the time of this writing, the dividend now sits at 18% and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. However, the chart below doesn't take the DRIP benefit into account here, which I will discuss later on.

Unlike most other high-yield funds, CLM isn't a simple buy-and-hold investment. I believe there are certain rules and structures you must follow in order to get the most out of your position. For instance, CLM offers a high dividend yield, but I'd argue that you shouldn't actually use any of the dividends received; In order to properly utilize the fund, you absolutely must reinvest distribution back down at the fund's NAV, which is how the real compounding magic happens. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying holdings of the fund.

Fund Holdings

What is unique about CLM is that the fund issues a large distribution while still holding traditional equities. The fund doesn't utilize any sort of fancy synthetic option writing strategies to generate the earnings it needs to fuel the distributions. Instead, CLM allocates a smaller portion of its total assets to different income-focused closed end funds to help boost the income that it generates. According to the latest semi-annual report for 2025, CLM allocates approximately 6.6% of assets to these income-focused assets, including the following:

  • Convertible Security Funds
  • Energy MLP Funds
  • Global Funds
  • Diversified Equity Funds
  • Income & Preferred Stock
  • Option Strategy Funds
  • Real Estate Focused Funds
  • Utilities Focused Funds

However, common stocks make up the overwhelming majority of the fund, so it makes sense to shift our focus there. Looking at the equities breakdown by sector, we get a well-rounded allocation that looks parallel to the S&P 500 (SPX). For instance, Technology holdings account for 30.45% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Consumer Cyclical companies, which account for 13.33% and 11.90% of assets respectively. Therefore, investors should anticipate CLM to follow the daily price movement of the S&P 500.

Looking at the top ten holdings below, we can see that CLM provides exposure to many of the highest quality companies in the world. For instance, Apple (AAPL) is the largest position with a weight of 7.42%. This is followed by other large-cap companies such as Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Tesla (TSLA) just to name a few. Since CLM also implements a generous distribution rate alongside these holdings, the fund has the ability to outperform traditional indexes.

Just to demonstrate this concept, I pulled in a performance chart of CLM over the last twelve months. As we can see below CLM's total return significantly outpaces the total return of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) over the last year. CLM has a total return of 28.17%, while SPY's total return is only 16.58%. When the fund is utilized correctly, I believe that investors can replicate this outperformance every single year.

The Blueprint

Since CLM operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying NAV. The main differentiating benefit of CLM is that all distributions received can be DRIP (dividend reinvestment plan) back down at the fund's NAV. As far as I know, CLM and Cornerstone Total Return Fund (CRF) are the only funds that offer this sort of benefit to investors. So if CLM is priced at $7.85 per share, but the NAV is worth $6.80 per share, all distributions will be automatically reinvested back down at the NAV. The fund's DRIP report lays out details about the process that I suggest reading through.

CLM's official net asset value table updates every week, but it's a quick way to reference how large of a premium CLM's price is trading compared to its NAV. As we can see below, the last updated table is from August 22nd, and states that the fund trades at a premium to NAV of 18.97%.

Step 1: Keep An Eye Out For Rights Offerings

When the fund's premium to NAV becomes too large, management implements a rights offering. When the fund trades at a large premium to NAV, management can capture that premium and turn it into cash by issuing new shares. A rights offering essentially allows investor to buy new shares at a closer price to its actual NAV. By issuing new shares and collecting cash, CLM can continue to grow its base assets under management year after year. These rights offerings are what causes CLM's share price to decline over the long-term.

The most recent rights offering was completed in May of 2025, so I don't anticipate another one taking place for at least another 12 months. The details of the rights offering were as follows:

Subscription Price

  1. 112% of NAV per share
  2. 80% of the market price per share

At the close of the offering, the formula resulted in a final price of $7.30 per share. These formulas are in place to ensure that CLM isn't selling shares for too cheap, but also to protect existing shareholders. To help conceptualize this, let's use the example below -

Suppose CLM has a NAV of $6.00 and the market price of $7.85 per share.

  • 112% of the NAV = $6.72 per share
  • 80% of the market price = $6.28 per share

The 112% is the higher of the two, so this will be the subscription price for the rights offering.

Step 2: Learn The Premium Thresholds

The most important step to utilizing CLM is to understand the premium to NAV thresholds. Rights offerings are typically announced when the premiums are very large. To help visualize this, I added red arrows to represent the periods when a rights offering was announced for CLM. Each time a rights offering was announced, the share price tends to see a large decline. So for me personally, I choose to sell out of my position as soon as I see a Form N-2 filed, which is an indication that a rights offering will be taking place.

For instance, the last N-2 filing was completed in February of 2025. Based on the historical performance, CLM sees a large decline right after the N-2 filing is revealed. For instance, I pulled in a chart of CLM's price on a YTD basis below. We can see how CLM saw a large decline near the end of February.

So the general rule of thumb that I follow is to only accumulate shares when CLM's premium to NAV is below 20%. Once the premium sits above 20% to its NAV, I stop accumulating shares and simply reinvest dividends. Once an N-2 filing is revealed, I immediately sell out of my position and then re-enter once the rights offering is completed.

Step 3: You Must Reinvest Distributions

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1224 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 18%. However, I believe that investors shouldn't actually withdraw or spend these distributions if they don't need to. Instead, I think it's more beneficial to reinvest distributions every single month. Since CLM now trades at a premium to NAV of around 19%, this means that all dividends being reinvested are receiving an instant 19% capital gain on all shares. So not only are you getting a high rate of income, but you are also compounding shares and expanding the equity of your position rapidly.

While I cannot confirm that all brokerages participate in this process, I have had no issues with Fidelity. Just to demonstrate, last month I received a distribution and it was automatically reinvested back down at the NAV. At the time, the NAV was around $6.85 per share, while the share price hovered above $8.00 per share at the time.

So in this sense, I don't think that CLM should be viewed as a traditional income position. Since rights offerings essentially dilute the fund over time, the share price and distribution payouts will consistently decline over time. That's why the dividend history below indicates that the payouts have declined over time. The most efficient way to offset this is to simply reinvest distributions over time and take advantage of the DRIP benefit.

Risks

CLM requires investors to actively manage their position and keep up with the filings. If you miss an N-2 filing and the share price collapses on you may see your capital erode over time. Additionally, I've been made aware by readers that some brokerages simply don't support the DRIP benefit. If your brokerage doesn't support reinvesting distributions back down at the NAV, you are missing a critical piece of the formula and I don't believe it's worth holding a position in the fund without it.

CLM will not be a good fit for investors that simply want to buy-and-hold through the next decade. Assuming several more rights offerings in the future, both the share price and payout amounts will continue their downward trends. If you aren't taking advantage of the DRIP benefit and simply holding, you are likely to underperform more traditional index tracking ETFs as well. As we can see below, CLM underperformed SPY over the last decade.

Lastly, the distributions received may cause tax consequences for investors over the long term. Looking at the semi-annual report for 2025 below, we can see that distributions were mostly fueled by the combination of net investment income, net realized gains, and return of capital. Net investment income is typically classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the highest tax burden. Net realized gains can technically be more tax-efficient if they are long-term capital gains but it is not specified in the reporting.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate CLM as a buy at this time due to the price trading at a premium to NAV under the 20% threshold. I plan to continue accumulating shares and reinvesting them back down at the NAV until the next N-2 filing, which I don't anticipate happening over the next year. The fund requires an active management style and I wouldn't suggest it for investors that want a buy-and-hold experience. Since the fund maintains exposure to equities that are representative of the S&P 500, CLM's daily price movements will generally match the indexes. So investors are still able to participate in market rallies with CLM, but will need to sell if the premium becomes too large and a rights offering is announced.

data.title: Supervielle: Moving Forward, But Still Far From Safe Ground
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In June, I noted that Supervielle (NYSE:SUPV) stood out for combining its banking tradition with an expanding digital model, supported by retail credit and InvertirOnline (IOL). That vision remains: The bank continues to show flexibility and innovation in a changing financial system.

Q2 reflected that two-sided view. The credit portfolio grew 71% YoY, real, net financial margin reached 20.8%, and ROAE returned positive at 5.8%. But at the same time, provisions rose 173% YoY and efficiency remains high, remembering that the bet on consumption drives growth, although it also exposes it to greater risks.

In valuation, SUPV is quoted with a discount against the sector (P/B FWD of 0.96x and P/CF TTM of 2.0x), and the consensus projects an EPS growth of more than 80%. However, the market still doubts that this profitability is sustainable.

The real challenge lies in the macro: positive real rates and abundance of treasury instruments competing with deposits and credit, an environment where Galicia (GGAL) or Macro (BMA) makes better use of its scale and diversification.

Therefore, regarding June, I adjust the vision. Supervielle still has solid foundations and an innovative proposal, but the context limits its ability to generate immediate value. The most prudent position today is a hold.

Q2 2025

Q2 2025 left signs of recovery for Supervielle. Attributable net profit reached AR$13.6B, an increase of 62% compared to the previous quarter, which closed H1 2025 with a positive ROAE of 5.8%. It does not yet reach Galicia or Macro, but marks an advance against the start of the year.

The net financial margin was one of the strong points, rising to 20.8% (+160 bp QoQ).

Credit portfolio grew 71% real YoY, with good performance in commercial loans and consumption as the main driver. They also accompanied deposits, which rose 42% YoY in real terms, with a jump of 154% in dollars, bringing the loan/deposit ratio to 71.7%, still below historical levels.

The other side was risk. Provisions increased 173%YoY and arrears reached 2.7%, still in line with the system, although more demanding than in banks with greater diversification. Efficiency was also affected: it rose to 60.9% from 50.6% a year ago, reflecting higher costs and investment in digitalization.

Even so, the operating cash was positive (AR$408MM), which allowed it to reinforce provisions and continue investing in technology. InvertirOnline already exceeds 600,000 customers and is consolidating as a key pillar in the revenue diversification strategy.

Valuation, Growth, and Profitability

Overall, Q2 2025 shows a SUPV that manages to grow, gain profitability, and keep arrears under control, but continues to pay the cost of its investment in consumption and a financial environment where positive real rates make it more expensive to fund and limit the speed of expansion. It is a bank that advances, although still with small steps, in a scenario that requires prudence.

The valuation and growth metrics reinforce the double reading that accompanies SUPV. On the one hand, it is traded with very low multiples: a P/B FWD of 0.96x and a P/CF TTM of just 2.0x, which places it well below the sector average. In terms of sales, it also shows a penalty, with a P/S FWD of 0.92x, almost 70% below the average. This suggests a strong market discount, though more out of mistrust than opportunity.

On the growth side, the contrast is striking: consensus projects a fall in EPS of 46.9% for 2025, followed by a very strong rebound in 2026 (+126.7%) and more stable growth in 2027 (+53.5%). That is, there is upside potential, but supported by extreme volatility.

In terms of profitability, the weaknesses are more exposed. The Net Income Margin barely reaches 6.2%, compared to a 22.8% sectoral, and the ROE of 5.9% is located at almost half of its peers. The ROA of 0.8% confirms the same: SUPV generates results, but still with limited returns on assets and capital.

Efficiency, at 60.9%, is still lower than that of large banks, which are around 50%. The only positive note is the strong cash generation (AR$408MM in the last 12 months), which allows it to sustain provisions and investments, although with a level of CAPEX on sales (7.4%) well above average, reflecting its digitization process.

Comparison With Peers

In market terms, SUPV stock showed an initial rally in 2025 but lagged behind GGAL and BMA in the July-August correction. The chart of Total Return YTD makes clear this dynamic: while GGAL and BBVA Argentina (BBAR) fell about 36% and BMA 40%, SUPV was the most punished, with a fall of more than 47%. This spread reflects the fact that, despite operational progress, the market still perceives SUPV as more exposed to consumer volatility and fragile demand for credit.

From a valuation point of view, the discount is obvious. SUPV is traded at less than 1.0x P/B and with cash flow multiples much lower than those of its peers, a sign that the market is still not confident in the sustainability of its returns. GGAL, on the other hand, manages to hold ratios closer to regional averages thanks to its scale and consistency, while BMA shows distortions in its accounting capital that make direct comparison more difficult.

The big difference is in profitability. The 5-year ROE chart makes clear the contrast: GGAL reaches almost 29%, BBVA remains at 16% and SUPV just 8%, while BMA shows virtually no return. This confirms that, even with margin and portfolio improvements, SUPV is one step behind in the ability to generate shareholder value.

Conclusion

The balance of Q2 makes it clear that Supervielle advances but still does not take off. Growing revenues, expanding portfolio, and improving financial margin, but paying the cost of increased exposure to consumption and a context where positive real rates continue to limit demand for credit. Profitability improves compared to the beginning of the year, but it is still far behind GGAL or BBVA, and the market does not reward its recovery with higher multiples.

Therefore, the thesis changes from my previous view. SUPV maintains solid fundamentals and an innovative model that combines traditional and digital banking, but the macro environment and volatility of its results justify caution. In this context, I consider that the most reasonable position is hold. Accompany the transformation and wait for a more benign macro scenario before considering SUPV as a real buying opportunity.

data.title: Intel: Some Hope Is Better Than None (Upgrade)
data.description:
Intel View Recap

I’ve been a longtime pessimist regarding Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) prospects, as I explained in an April article. The reasons are many, but the most important is that falling behind in the foundry business tends to create a death spiral.

The bigger you are at the leading edge, the more economies of scale you have and the better your data to forecast demand and adjust capex and pricing for the next product cycle. More revenue and higher margins also allow bigger capex. Losing that leadership is hard.

Intel also has little experience producing for third parties, and a lot of adaptation is needed to address different needs from different customers. In that model, reputation matters to capture clients whose businesses depend on timely deliveries.

All of this has left Intel’s new foundry business with virtually no clients on the AI front, and the last earnings call was the first time the company openly admitted it may leave the 14A process if no clients are landed.

To make things even worse, the foundry problem has also led to a continuous loss of market share in both the server and PC markets, and that is the business expected to fund the new foundry initiative.

In this article, I’ll address the last earnings and comment on the news about the government taking an equity stake in Intel, and why I’m upgrading my Sell rating.

Intel Q2 2025 Earnings

The company’s revenue was better than consensus (12.9 billion vs. 11.9 billion), slightly up YoY and 1.5% up sequentially. It was the first YoY growth quarter since Q1 2024. As I’ve discussed in some of my latest earnings comments, I do believe we’re seeing some demand pulled in in response to tariffs, both at AMD and Intel, helping their Client Computing Groups. Management wouldn’t rule out this effect.

EPS was far from consensus mainly because of two distinct categories of significant one-time costs:

  • A $1.9 billion restructuring charge primarily related to planned headcount reductions of approximately 15% of its core workforce. This charge alone had an impact of $(0.45) on GAAP EPS.
  • A separate $800 million non-cash impairment and accelerated depreciation charge for excess manufacturing tools, combined with $200 million in one-time period costs. These charges, totaling $1.0 billion, impacted both GAAP and non-GAAP EPS by approximately $(0.23) and $(0.20), respectively.

This was the second earnings call from the new CEO, Lip-Bu, who at least provided a realistic overview of the challenges ahead. I like this way of addressing the problems and the markets much more than I did under Pat. More reality and less “united we stand / the future is bright.” That’s just my view.

One passage worth calling out from the call:

I do not subscribe to the belief that if you build it, they will come. Under my leadership, we will build what customers need, when they need it and earn their trust through consistent execution.

I like this positioning because it’s the correct diagnosis. In a classic Innovator’s Dilemma story, Intel had decades of success in x86 processors, which led to big and sustainable margins that shaped culture and decisions. The company rarely had to worry about what the market needed; it mainly dictated incremental innovations within its own process. That’s part of why it lost the mobile market, with the iPhone ultimately eating a big part of the notebook market.

Management also talked about the possibility of not seeking further nodes after 18A, which I briefly cited in the introduction.

Up to and through Intel 18A, we could generate a reasonable return on our investments with only Intel products. The increase in capital cost at Intel 14A makes it clear that we need both Intel products and a meaningful external customer to drive acceptable returns on our deployed capital, and I will only invest when I’m confident those returns exist.

I see that as a smart move to signal to the U.S. government that direct help may be needed. Politically, I don’t think the Trump administration can allow that outcome.

Overall, the situation has not changed much. Intel still suffers from losing reputation and leadership, needs cash for capex, and is struggling to get customers. The server side of x86 is still bleeding share to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). At least Lip-Bu has shifted the focus to a more conservative one, admitting that costs and capex were excessive and scaling some of them back while conveying seriousness.

The stock reaction to the earnings call was poor, with a drop of almost 8%, and it only rebounded after two pieces of news, the most important being the American government taking a 10% stake at the company.

The News

The first was a deal signed with SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) for a 2 billion dollar stake, with others being considered. No surprise that Intel needs lots of money.

The more controversial item was the deal the government struck for 10% of equity. After some speculation, this came short of investor expectations, as it is actually no new money but a stake in exchange for old money:

Financially, it’s not good: dilution for money that was already theirs. But the stock rebounded on that news for a simple reason: the government now has skin in the game.

I think this signals that the foundry business isn’t going anywhere and its failure will not be allowed by the Oval Office.

Can the government act to guarantee new clients for the company? That’s speculation for now and Scott Bessant denied that interpretation, but I don’t buy it. Let’s remember that this same administration has required both AMD and Nvidia (NVDA) to pay 15% on revenues from products exported to China and obliged TSMC (TSM) to invest heavily in American fabs.

I believe the administration could force AMD, Nvidia, and Broadcom (AVGO) to allocate some orders with Intel, while also pushing big techs to invest, and even making equipment vendors like KLA (KLAC), Lam Research (LRCX), Applied Materials (AMAT) and software companies like Cadence (CDNS) and Synopsys (SNPS) help Intel become a third-party manufacturer because they have the know-how to serve that market.

I admit that’s largely speculative, but a spark of hope is better than none. And as for the share dilution, a bigger share in a bankrupt company would worth less by default.

Conclusion

I’m not a fan of Intel’s situation over the last, let’s say, five years, but I believe this is the first time there’s a spark of hope, even if highly speculative. I don’t typically change my ratings without a fundamental shift in financials, but with a Sell rating I think sentiment and momentum deserve more weight.

The problem for INTC was never just cash, but know-how and culture, and I think there’s a chance the government forces the American semi ecosystem to work for them. For that reason, I’m upgrading my Sell rating to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: What'll It Take To Get These Commodity Stocks Moving?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Mining shares have been on fire all year long. But what will it take to get a different batch of commodity stocks moving? I’m talking, of course, about energy names.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the year-to-date performance of the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), First Trust Natural Gas ETF (FCG), SPDR Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (XOP), and the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH).

XLE, FCG, XOP, OIH (YTD % Change)

(Data by YCharts)

While there is some overlap in holdings, each ETF focuses on different names and subsectors in the crude oil and natural gas businesses. XLE is heavily into mega-cap, multinational, diversified energy names, while FCG holds more gas-heavy producers and transportation companies. Yet, the performance overall is similar: lackluster. It ranges from -6% for OIH to +4.7% for XLE.

What’s the culprit? Concern that too much oil supply is hitting the market at a time when demand growth remains tepid.

This year, OPEC+ members have largely rescinded output cuts that totaled 2.2 million barrels per day (BPD). Overall global supply is expected to grow another 1.9 million BPD next year, according to the International Energy Agency. Meanwhile, global consumption is expected to rise just 700,000 BPD to 104.4 million BPD in 2026.

In short, the supply-demand picture is much cloudier in the energy space than it is in the precious metals market. But as the saying goes, the best cure for low prices is... low prices. We’ll likely see drilling and production activity cool if oil and gas prices remain subdued. That, in turn, will eventually boost investor interest in the sector.

So, if you’re looking for something that hasn’t run yet in the resources sector... or that might offer compelling value... you might want to check out some of the energy funds mentioned here.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alibaba's Big Comeback: Why Q1 Results Weren't A 'Miss'
data.description:
Double miss for Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF), with Q1 2026 EPS at $2.06 ($0.10 less than expected) and revenues reaching $34.57 billion ($910 million less than forecast).

At first glance, these results may seem terrible, but I will show you how this fiscal Q1 was actually very positive overall. The market shares this opinion, and the stock is jumping 3% in pre-market trading.

Q1 2025 Highlights

To better understand why this quarterly report was a success, I think a little context is needed.

Under the leadership of Eddie Wu (September 2023), Alibaba has begun a gradual process of reducing waste and focusing on its core businesses. The Alibaba of the past was a highly diversified giant, perhaps too much so, as it was losing its competitive advantage in its core segments; too many unnecessary investments and, above all, low-margin and low-growth businesses.

With Eddie Wu, the tune has changed, and Alibaba is selling some of its historic but low-potential businesses quarter after quarter. Among the most important are Sun Art, sold for $1.70 billion, and Intime, sold for $1.02 billion. These businesses were no longer attractive to management, but their sale clearly had an impact on financial statements. Although low-margin, they were still capable of generating billions of dollars in revenue.

If we look at Q1 revenue growth, including Sun Art and Intime, it would only be 2%. However, excluding them from the calculation (which I believe is more accurate), YoY revenue growth rises to 10%. So Alibaba is back to double-digit growth.

Don't give too much weight to the collapse in cash from operations, as it is heavily impacted by multiple disposals. Furthermore, free cash flow is strongly negative as CapEx has more than tripled YoY. Alibaba is investing heavily in AI services and recently also in its core business, China Commerce.

While the divestment strategy is limiting sales growth on paper, it is leading to a significant improvement in the cost structure.

Cost of revenue and administrative expenses significantly reduced their weight relative to revenue, while product development and marketing expenses increased.

That's what happened in general. Now let's take a look at how each individual operating segment performed in Q1 FY2026.

China E-commerce

China E-commerce remains Alibaba's most important segment and has finally returned to double-digit growth. Revenues increased by 10% YoY, thanks in particular to quick commerce and China commerce wholesale, up 12% and 13%, respectively.

Adjusted EBITA fell by 21% YoY, and although this may seem like a bad result at first glance, it is not. It mainly depends on the significant investments made in Taobao Instant Commerce, Alibaba's service that allows users to buy online and receive the product in a few hours.

By leveraging Cainiao's logistics chain, Alibaba can offer an almost instant e-commerce service that users are really enjoying. Just think that in the first three weeks of August, active users on Taobao increased by 25% and 88VIP members (users who spend the most) grew by double digits to over 53 million.

The Taobao Instant Commerce service will require significant investment, but its potential is huge. Consider that users can also purchase food through it and have it delivered directly to their homes in a short time. This could see Alibaba dethrone Meituan, China's leading food delivery company.

International Digital Commerce Group

International commerce is a high-growth segment and includes platforms such as AliExpress, Trendyol, and Lazada.

Sales grew by 20% YoY, but the biggest news concerns a marked improvement in operational efficiency. In fact, adjusted EBITA is close to completely eliminating the loss.

In addition to selling products themselves, Alibaba is making the most of other ways to monetize through its apps, with advertising a key part of this strategy:

Our international wholesale platform saw broader adoption by merchants of its AI-powered tools for marketing, procurement and product listing, which provided multiple ways for the platform to monetize.

Cloud Intelligence

Alibaba's Cloud is not its most important business, but it is certainly the one with the highest potential.

Revenue growth continues to accelerate and has reached 26% YoY. Adjusted EBITA grew at the same pace, which is perhaps the only flaw, as I expected a greater improvement in profitability. After all, we are talking about a highly scalable business, and EBIT tends to grow faster than revenue.

In any case, I remain satisfied with the overall performance, and management is focusing heavily on the Cloud in terms of CapEx. Demand for AI services is insane and has grown by triple digits for the eighth quarter in a row:

AI-related product revenue maintained triple-digit year-over-year growth for the eighth consecutive quarter. As AI demand continues to grow rapidly, we are also seeing increased demand of compute, storage and other public cloud services to support AI adoption. We will continue to invest in anticipation of customer growth and technology innovation, including AI products and services, to increase cloud adoption for AI and maintain our market leadership.

With such strong demand, the billions invested are almost certain to yield a solid return. In fact, I believe Alibaba's main problem is that it has invested too little in its cloud infrastructure in the past. Now it needs to catch up and grow its cloud revenues by more than 30% if not more.

All Others

All Others is the segment that includes the remaining businesses, and there is no particular news in this regard. Freshippo, Amap, Hujing DME, and Alibaba Health achieved operational improvements, but the sale of Sun Art and Intime weighed on the final result. Unlike in previous quarterly reports, the company also included Cainiao in this segment; its results were probably negative once again and were no longer relevant enough to mention separately.

Although it generates a large amount of revenue, All Others is Alibaba's weakest segment, but the positive aspect is that management is aware of this. Over time, it is reducing its weight, and I expect further divestments in the future.

Conclusion

Overall, I consider this quarterly report to be positive, and as a shareholder, I am very satisfied with it. I wanted a greater focus on the core businesses, and that is what we got; I wanted Alibaba to return to double-digit growth, and it succeeded (excluding Sun Art and Intime); I wanted the Cloud to exceed a growth rate of +20%, and it succeeded.

For the next quarter, I expect Cloud to grow above 30% (AI services are starting to weigh heavily on its revenues) and, above all, for China Commerce to continue to expand at double-digit rates. On the International side, I would be very pleased if it finally generated positive EBITA. Further divestments of inefficient businesses are welcome, especially if the profits are reinvested in Cloud.

As for buybacks, the company has slowed down the purchase of its own shares.

Not everyone may agree with this decision, but I do. There are too many important investment opportunities, and it would be a waste not to take advantage of them just to reduce the number of shares in circulation. Furthermore, the price per share is no longer $80, and this affects the effectiveness of the buyback.

Either way, I still consider Alibaba to be significantly undervalued; a company like this cannot have a forward P/E ratio of only 14x. At the very least, it has the potential to increase EPS by 10-15% over the next 5-10 years, and this estimate is probably too conservative. If it were a U.S. company, it would have a forward P/E ratio of at least 20-25x, in line with the major American AI-focused tech companies. Up to $150 per share, it remains a strong buy in my opinion.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Yellow Cake: Analysis Of Uranium Supply And Demand
data.description:
Introduction

I usually tend to avoid analyzing or investing in companies linked to raw materials; however, I would like to make an exception with this article. Recently, I have become interested in the world behind nuclear energy production, delving into the entire uranium supply chain and the main companies operating in it. I would therefore like to use the analysis of Yellow Cake plc (YLLXF) to explore these issues in greater depth and understand whether investing in uranium is an opportunity.

Business Model

Yellow Cake's business model is very simple: the company purchases and holds long-term stocks of uranium (U₃O₈). The purchase of uranium is financed by the sale of shares, which usually takes place when the share price is equal to or higher than the NAV. The company has an agreement to purchase uranium worth up to $100 million per year from Kazatomprom (the world's leading uranium producer) until the end of 2027. The stocks are ultimately held in Cameco and Orano's storage facilities in Canada and France, respectively.

An investment in Yellow Cake offers direct exposure to uranium price trends. The main ETFs that invest in the uranium sector do so by purchasing companies involved in the production chain, such as mining companies or companies that develop technologies related to this sector. If you want direct exposure to uranium price trends, Yellow Cake is the best option, as investing in companies that extract it also exposes investors to exploration risk and other operational risks typical of a mining company. However, it should also be noted that Yellow Cake does not generate any cash flow for its shareholders, and that investing in companies that extract a specific raw material offers leveraged exposure to the price of that raw material, which may be appreciated by some investors.

Another aspect that needs to be addressed is management compensation policy and Yellow Cake's "TER", or the ratio of operating expenses to book value. In the first image, you can see the five main uranium-related ETFs, with an average TER of 0.67%. For comparison, in the second image, I have created a table showing the trend in Yellow Cake's operating expenses and equity from 2019 to 2025, with an average of 0.92%; we can therefore say that the management cost is higher than that of ETFs.

At this point, I believe it is appropriate to analyze the company's Long Term Incentive Plan [LTIP] to understand whether the management incentive metrics are reasonable or not. First, it should be noted that only the CEO and CFO have access to this incentive plan and receive free options with a vesting period of three years. The allocation of options is subject to two criteria:

  • Performance of Yellow Cake's share price relative to a basket of comparables (80% of valuation):
  • comparison with Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (15%),
  • b. comparison with the spot price of U3O8 uranium (15%),
  • c. comparison with a basket of 3 uranium ETFs (50%).

Relative performance determines the vesting level (100%, 50%, or none).

  • Material growth in uranium inventories and group revenues (20% of the assessment).

The three ETFs used for comparison are: Global X Uranium ETF (URA), Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM), and Sprott Junior Uranium Miners ETF (URNJ).

The terms of the incentive plan seem reasonable to me and, even though the cost is higher than that of ETFs, I believe that the risk of pay-without-performance is reduced, given that managers' compensation is linked to a benchmark that is consistent and appropriate to the company's business.

That said, in order to understand whether or not to invest in Yellow Cake, it is necessary to understand the uranium market and the factors that drive the supply and demand for this resource, given that the value of this company is exclusively linked to the value of its uranium reserves.

Uranium Supply

As you can imagine, uranium production is an extremely critical and internationally controlled activity, and although uranium is a globally distributed resource, actual production is heavily concentrated in a few countries and depends largely on political and economic decisions. At every level of the supply chain, the market is controlled by a few companies, the main ones with state participation.

Kazakhstan is the world's leading producer of uranium (43% of global production), followed by Canada (15%) and Namibia (12%). The mining companies that dominate the market are therefore Kazatomprom (Kazakhstan), Cameco (Canada), and Orano (France, a group active in the entire fuel cycle and a producer accounting for 9-11% of global production). Kazakhstan is also the country with the largest uranium reserves and the lowest extraction costs.

After the long pause in nuclear development following the Fukushima accident, uranium production began to recover in 2023, reaching 54.500 tU, an increase of 12% compared to 2020 levels. Following the improvement in market conditions, some mines undergoing maintenance were reactivated to ensure supply to the growing fleet of nuclear power plants globally. Recent uncertainties affecting the market include the Nigerian authorities' revocation in July 2023 of Orano's permit to exploit the Imouraren deposit, followed in June 2024 by the revocation of GaviEx Uranium (Canada-based) for the Madaouela project, creating significant uncertainty regarding future production centers.

Leaving aside these unforeseen events, many producers have expanded production in recent years. At the same time, governments are developing new frameworks in countries that previously had no uranium production. It should be noted that opening or expanding a uranium mine is not a simple undertaking and requires a lot of time, with many variables that can slow down the launch of these projects.

All investor presentations by uranium-related companies state that there is a gap between production and demand, which will lead to an increase in the price of uranium. However, as indicated by the Nuclear Energy Agency (NEA), this gap between production and demand has existed since 1990 and has been consistently filled by secondary supplies, primarily consisting of reprocessed fuel, stockpiles, and disarmament materials from nuclear weapons programs. The NEA report highlights that although this gap has begun to close in recent years, projections indicate that it could widen again, given the expected increase in demand in the coming years. Although the presence of inventories, including military stockpiles, will continue to represent an element of uncertainty for future investments, reactor requirements will increasingly need to be met by primary production.

In its latest report (Uranium 2024: Resources, Production, and Demand), the NEA identifies two possible scenarios:

  • Low-production scenario: If all existing and committed mines operate at or near their stated production capacity, low demand is expected to be met until 2031. However, for high demand, a production shortfall is anticipated to emerge around 2027.
  • High-production scenario: If planned and prospective production capabilities are included, high demand could be met by primary production until 2037. In this scenario, low demand would be satisfied until 2042.

Uranium Demand

As of January 1, 2023, there were 438 commercial nuclear reactors in operation worldwide and 58 under construction. A significant increase in the number of nuclear reactors is expected in the coming decades, which are essential for producing a stable, low-cost energy source and reducing emissions. In 2022, the European Commission included nuclear energy in its EU taxonomy, classifying it as an environmentally sustainable investment that will help decarbonize the European Union's economy. The countries investing most heavily in nuclear power are India and China. Alongside these developments are the US, which remains the largest user of reactors; France, which is heavily dependent on nuclear power and has launched strategies to maintain and renew its existing fleet; and Japan, which is gradually bringing back into operation the reactors shut down after Fukushima.

In the short-term, the quantities of uranium required for reactor operation can be predicted with a high degree of certainty. Generally, higher uranium prices have encouraged utilities to reduce uranium requirements by specifying lower tails assays at enrichment facilities, to the extent possible. In recognition of these market trends, uranium requirements for the operational lifetime of projected new reactors have been reduced from 175 tU/GWe/yr to 160 tU/GWe/yr (from a 0.30% to a 0.25% tails assay). When determining the quantities of uranium that will be needed, it should also be taken into account that first load fuel requirements are around 60% higher than reloads for plants in operation. In addition, the excellent performance of currently operating reactors has led to a trend toward extending their useful life. The tables below show the NEA's estimates for installed capacity and the quantities of uranium needed to meet it until 2050. In 2050, installed capacity is expected to be 2.3 times that of 2021, and the uranium needed is expected to be about 2.4 times that of 2022.

One factor that introduces uncertainty into these forecasts is undoubtedly possible technological developments, such as the future adoption of fourth-generation reactors, which promise to be able to use waste from old power plants as fuel. However, the reactors currently under construction use “traditional” technologies that have been in use for decades and are known to be reliable.

Conclusion

Yellow Cake is an excellent way to invest in uranium in a “pure” way, without exposure to the operational risks typical of mining companies. In light of the outlook for uranium supply and demand in the coming years, it is reasonable to assume a structural increase in the price of this metal, positively influencing the value of Yellow Cake's reserves and its NAV. However, given the absence of cash flows, it is extremely difficult to value the company, and in my opinion, this does not make it a good option for a classic long-term investment.

Another risk to consider is that it is unclear what will happen after the contract with Kazatomprom expires at the end of 2027. Yellow Cake may find it difficult to negotiate a new supply agreement, meaning that the company's uranium reserves could remain stable. Clearly, this does not reduce the value of the company, but the mechanism adopted so far of issuing shares when their price is above NAV in order to buy more uranium could be difficult to continue.

In conclusion, considering current reserves, the GBP/USD exchange rate, the price of uranium, and Yellow Cake's share price at the time of writing, the share price is trading at a discount of approximately 9% to NAV.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PDI: I Remain Bullish About This Income Fund
data.description:
Since I started covering the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI), there has been a bear case discussing the liquidity of PDI’s distribution and that they wouldn’t be able to maintain it throughout an inflationary environment. PDI has ridden out the storm, is up 2.04% over the past year, and has paid a monthly distribution of $0.23 since September 2015. When I look for income-producing assets I am on the hunt for quality income that comes with a strong long-term investment case. PIMCO is a global leader in active fixed income, and it is much easier to gain exposure to these markets through PDI and let their management team do the work. I believe that shares of PDI will continue to generate large amounts of recurring income and as the macroeconomic environment shifts PDI’s underlying assets should experience significant appreciation as bond prices should increase as yields fall. Another positive is that since PDI is a leveraged fund it should benefit from improved borrowing costs as the Fed lowers rates. Many investors have been waiting a long time for Fed Chair Powell to pivot and in the latest speech from Jackson Hole I believe we received the signal that the Fed will shift on monetary policy in September. As the risk-free rate of return starts to decline again allocating capital toward risk-free assets such as money markets and CDs will look less appealing and PDI’s distribution yield of 13.61% will look very enticing. I am long PDI, and reinvesting the monthly income has allowed me to organically build out a stronger income stream each month.

Following up on my previous article about PDI

Since my previous article about PDI (can be read here) shares have appreciated by 5.3% while the S&P 500 has increased by 11.31%. When the monthly distributions are accounted for PDI’s total return over this period has been 9.01%. I had discussed why PDI’s 14% yield was still attractive despite its share price declines. I felt that PDI’s focus on agency mortgages and dynamic asset allocation provided a level of resilience and that its actively managed fixed-income portfolio is well-positioned for a rebound. PDI continued to deliver large amounts of income on a monthly basis, and I am more bullish today than I previously was. We are one step closer to rate cuts now that Fed Chair Powell delivered his address from Jackson Hole and I am following up with a new article to discuss why I feel PDI is going to rally into the end of the year and continue pumping out large amounts of income.

Risks to investing in PDI

While I am bullish on PDI, there are several risk factors that investors should consider. PDI is a leveraged CEF, which makes it sensitive to interest rates, credit events, and macroeconomic shifts. If, for some reason, something occurs in the data to make the Fed change their stance on monetary policy, it could be very negative for PDI as it could lead to declines in bond prices and the underlying NAV. PDI employs a leverage ratio that exceeds 30% and in a higher-rate environment the chances of its underlying holdings experiencing higher rates of defaults or credit downgrades increase significantly. If companies in their portfolio experience hardships, then PDI’s distribution could face coverage shortfalls as some of its assets could turn illiquid and disrupt the strong distribution yield. Investors should do their own due diligence as it’s difficult to research PDI’s underlying assets and a lot of faith is being placed on the management team. Investors should also be aware that PDI focuses on fixed income, and its share price is unlikely to replicate the returns of an S&P 500 index fund.

Fed Chair Powell just signaled that rate cuts are coming in my opinion and that is bullish for PDI

In Fed Chair Powell's speech from 8/22, he addressed the economic situation and the near-term outlook for monetary policy. Fed Chair Powell stated that “with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.” Throughout the address he discussed the current economic situation and the near-term outlook for monetary policy, but all roads led back to the idea that adjusting their policy stance may be necessary. This was enough for CME Group to update the chances of a rate cut at the September meeting to 86.2% while getting 2 rate cuts by the end of the year increased to 48.3% and getting 3 rate cuts increased to 37.3%. Looking out to the end of 2026, the most likely scenario is that rates would fall 225 bps from where they are today which is super bullish for PDI. This would create a night and day difference in the macroeconomic environment which would have drastic implications for fixed income.

If we do get a prolonged Fed cutting cycle, I think that we’re going to see PDI’s share price get back to the mid-20s. PDI predominantly invests in global fixed income securities which include high-yield bonds, mortgage-backed securities (MBS), emerging market debt, and other credit instruments. When interest rates decline the prices of existing fixed income securities typically increase because their higher coupon payments become more attractive relative to newly issued bonds with lower yields. PDI’s portfolio includes a mix of investment-grade and below investment-grade bonds that would benefit from the inverse relationship between interest rates and bond prices. An ongoing cutting cycle like the one CME Group is projecting would likely result in a sustained bond rally that would increase PDI’s NAV over time. PDI’s portfolio also has interest rate-sensitive assets such as MBS and corporate bonds, and a lower rate environment would reduce the discount rates used to value future cash flow.

PDI has a large allocation toward bonds which are debt securities that pay a fixed interest rate over their term. The yield on a bond is the return an investor will earn through its payments, which is calculated as the annual coupon payment divided by the bond's current market price. When interest rates fall the yield on newly issued bonds decreases because they are priced to reflect the lower rate environment. PDI’s portfolio of existing bonds will continue paying their higher coupon rates which makes them more attractive to investors compared to new bonds with lower yields. The inverse relationship will cause older bonds that traded at a discount to increase in value as investors buy them until their effective yield correlates to newly issued bonds. I believe that this will take PDI’s share price into the mid $20 range over the next 6-12 months. When I look at PDI’s share performance, there is a direct correlation to a higher rate environment and PDI’s share price falling. I am expecting a breakout to occur as rates fall under 4% to the upside.

PDI’s distribution yield is very lucrative and much more effective than traditional income funds

I believe that PIMCO has built a better income machine through PDI than many investment vehicles on the market. PDI went IPO for $25 in 2012, and since then it has produced $41.62 of distribution income which is 166.47% of its share price. Not only have investors received their entire investment back, but they have also generated an additional $16.62 per share in distributions while the shares are still producing $2.65 of annualized income. This is where my income-focused investing style comes into play because I am not concerned about a -22.08% drop overtime in the share price considering that the investment has already been paid for from the income for long-term investors. It’s hard to get upset about a fluctuating share price that is spending a few years under its IPO price when the main investment objective is to generate recurring distribution income. PDI has never cut the distribution and provided investors with several raises over the years. I still think this is a great opportunity to lock in a 13.61% distribution yield, as I believe PDI’s share price will increase as the Fed lowers rates.

There is less reason from an income perspective to invest in an ETF such as the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM) that is yielding 2.54% with an average 5-year dividend growth rate of 3.59%. If I were to put $100,000 into VYM, I could purchase 719.22 shares. If I held it for 6 years, I would generate $2,538.84 of dividend income in year 1 and the dividend would grow to $4.21, which is an increase of 19.29% ($0.68). The annual dividend would increase to $3,023.69, and over the 6-year period of the $100,000 investment there would be $16,645.10 of dividend income generated. PDI, on the other hand, would generate $13,603.70 of distribution income off a $100,000 investment in the first year and if the distribution doesn’t increase over the 6-year period PDI would generate $81,622.18 in distributions. I think there is less reason to purchase lower-yielding equity-based ETFs because from an income perspective, the dividend growth from VYM doesn’t correlate to enough income generated to get excited. I think that as the Fed lowers rates, we’re going to see a lot of capital move into funds like PDI because of their income-generating potential and I am adding to my position before the share price gets back into the $20s.

Conclusion

Fed Chair Powell has spoken, and he clearly indicated that adjusting their policy stance is on the table due to the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks. This is very bullish for PDI because most of its underlying assets will greatly benefit causing their NAV to increase and pushing PDI’s share price higher. I think that shares of PDI have the potential to get back into the mid $20s as the Fed takes its rate slower while maintaining their current distribution rate. From an income perspective, there is less reason to look at low single-digit yielding ETFs for income compared to a fund like PDI that is comprised of global fixed income assets with double-digit yields. I think that PDI can generate large amounts of recurring income and large monthly distributable income for its shareholders, and I am very bullish heading into the remainder of 2025.

data.title: Free Cash Flow As A Proxy For Quality In Europe: Introducing The S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats Index
data.description:
By Elizabeth Bebb

The S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats® Index introduces the quality free cash flow (FCF) methodology to the European region. The index methodology selects high quality companies in the S&P Europe LargeMidCap universe that emphasize consistent and efficient free cash flow generation.1 Our recent education paper explores the use of FCF metrics in creating indices with quality factor characteristics.

As with the existing S&P Quality FCF Aristocrats Indices, the new European index starts by selecting those companies that have 10 years of positive FCF generation, including positive FCF for the last 12 months. The constituents are then selected using the FCF score metrics, FCF ROIC and FCF margin, and weighted by float market cap multiplied by FCF score.

Over the long term back-tested period, the index outperformed its benchmark by 150 bps since April 30, 2006, while also demonstrating lower volatility with reduced drawdowns. The historical capture ratio also demonstrates the index’s upside potential with defensiveness against downside risk.

Over the period studied, in the majority of cases when the market saw large drawdowns, the S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats Index saw a lower drawdown than the benchmark. The most noticeable of these was during the onset of COVID-19, when the index only matched approximately 50% of the market fall realized by the benchmark.

As of the most recent rebalance, the index had higher tilts toward the U.K., Switzerland, Denmark and the Netherlands compared to the benchmark, which has more weight in Germany and Southern European markets.

Active sector weights are shown in Exhibit 5. Historically, there were higher allocations on average to Health Care, Consumer Staples, Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology, with a significant tilt away from Financials and Energy.

More recently, the weights in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology increased significantly, showing higher weights compared to the benchmark. Meanwhile, the weights to the Consumer Staples and Health Care sectors decreased. Underweights in Financials2 and Energy remained significant.

The Magnificent 7 stocks have been the topic of much discussion for their impact on the S&P 500. In Europe, the 11 companies that have led market performance are known as the “Granolas.” The index has representation in almost all these companies (excluding Nestlé) and has more than double the weight of the underlying benchmark, recognizing the high FCF scores and quality of these companies over the long term (see Exhibit 6).

The S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats Index is the newest addition to the S&P Aristocrats Index Suite. This regional index has demonstrated a strong quality tilt across its back-tested history, providing a benchmark that uses FCF as proxy for long-term quality in the European market.

1 The S&P 500® Quality FCF Aristocrats Index and S&P Developed Quality FCF Aristocrats Index were both launched in December 2024.

2 Some Financials stocks are excluded due to the availability of metrics (see methodology). All Real Estate sector constituents are excluded.
